name: Al Gallodoro
abstract: Alfred J. Gallodoro, (June 20, 1913 – October 4, 2008) was an American jazz clarinetist and saxophonist, who performed from the 1920s up until his death.

interviews.title: Al Gallodoro interviewed by Monk Rowe, Oneonta, New York, December 28, 2005
Spiegle Willcox and Al Gallodoro interviewed by Monk Rowe, Norwich, New York, November 14, 1995

quotes.text: I did the Paul Whiteman Rediscovered. Around 1977. We opened at the Academy of Music in Philadelphia, standing room, two days later we went to Carnegie Hall, standing room, we were supposed to go on the road and nothing happened. © Fillius Jazz Archive -8-
quotes.caption: Al Gallodoro recalls performing at Paul Whiteman Rediscovered in 1976
quotes.doc_title: Spiegle Willcox and Al Gallodoro interviewed by Monk Rowe, Norwich, New York, November 14, 1995

quotes.text: You have it. Well we hired a 50 piece orchestra. We each put up $1,350. The session started right in Carnegie Hall at midnight. And we went until six in the morning. And I did the whole three movements of the “Concerto for Doubles.” Alto sax, bass clarinet, clarinet. Then I did “Oodles of Noodles” and the “Fantasy Impromptu.” But I was in my prime then. 50 piece orchestra. My brother Frank was in the band. And I went to the office one day and he gave me a check for about $240. He says, “I’m going to send Ralph his money” because Ralph went back to Milwaukee. That’s where he was from, Milwaukee, Wisconsin. But Ralph never received any money. They were scoundrels, I’ll tell you right now. They absconded with the money. They absconded with the original platter. Ralph could never get it back. Sam, Irving Berman was the other fellow. He was on the Unfair List for about two or three years in the New York Union newspaper. Unfair List. So that’s the story on the “Concerto for Doubles.” Well those records were a little scratchy. Now I have that in my “Many Sides.” Do you have that? “The Many Sides of Al Gallodoro?”
quotes.caption: Recording Concerto for Doubles with 50-piece orchestra
quotes.doc_title: Al Gallodoro interviewed by Monk Rowe, Oneonta, New York, December 28, 2005

quotes.text: Well, I suppose I'll tell them about the Toscanini incident. Yeah, come on. Well, you've heard of Paul LaValle, the band leader, Paul LaValle. Well, I was working an ice show. Sonya Henney and Arthur Works opened up this ice show. They weren't in the show. Like the shows that you see now on tour, the ice shows, Holiday on the Ice and so forth. All right. Well, we opened the original. And Paul LaValle at that time was at NBC. And he says, Al, he says, how would you like to go with Toscanini? I said, gee, I don't know. I says, I played bass clarinet with Whiteman. I'm not afraid of the parts, but how about the bass clap? I didn't know the bass clap, you know. Well, I knew the bass clap, but only to transpose it like a cello part, a tone higher. But in the symphony, when they write the bass clarinet, they write it in your class. They write it in your key. In other words, the second space is a C, right? Now, if it's a cello part, I would have to play a D because this is B flat instrument. But they don't write it that way. They write, when they write a C, it's already transposed for you. So therefore, you play a C on your bass clarinet. So anyway, I took out my books. I have these recording books with passages from the Puccini operas and so forth. And man, I'm practicing up on them, you know. And Dr. Frank Black was a big conductor. He says, Al, you're playing these a tone too high. He says, you're playing the mezzo soprano, I believe. So they had to write me the Tristani's oldie in my key, right. So anyway, I says, I'm not worrying about that in a few weeks. I'll get it down. So here I go on stage 8H. I'll never forget it. Toscanine is in the audience. Dr. Frank Black and H. Leopold's Bittani. And there I am sitting on this big stage, all in all, with the bass clarinet, playing off of this and playing off of that. Well, I want to tell you. If you ever got scared, I was scared. I was scared because here was the master, you know. But I played a few things written out and within two weeks, you know what I did? I played smart. You have to play smart. You just go out and get yourself trombone books, bassoon books, anything in bass clef, and you just take it easy. And within a couple of weeks, I knew the bass clef. That's one story of my life. I was there two and a half years. I'd like to play something for you from the Tristan and Isolde solo. Now, if you notice, I played this all in that same register, in the same neighborhood. If you played... Of course, I do it pretty smooth, you know. But still, it's a different change. So, I decided to keep it all in this register. I have to tell you this. It's not bragging because it actually happened. We were doing a Red Cross benefit at Carnegie Hall with Toscanini. And this was the first time we had played this after I joined the symphony. And we played it and Misha Misha Koffer was the concertmaster. After I got through playing it, Toscanini looks down to Misha Misha Koffer and says, like this, you know. I thought to myself, oh my God, what happened? I thought I played it well. So, after Misha comes to me, doing a five-minute break, he says, you know what the old band said about you? He said, that's the first time we ever heard that passage played in tune. This is the truth. Because when you jump to the C, it's have to be sharp. The C is have to be sharp on the bass clarinet. But here, if you tune them right, you have better control. So, I thought I'd pass that along if you could ever play it, try and conquer it this way. Then, let's see the other part. Oh yes, the high notes. I use my own, how many bass clarinet players here? Many? None here.
quotes.caption: Al Gallodoro recalls playing bass clarinet with Toscanini

quotes.text: Son of a gun. I'll play for you tomorrow night. Oh my God, it's a collector's item. I can go on and on. Take it on tape, that's all right. We got it. All right. Whiteman commissioned this for me and the reason it was called concerto for doubles in the band, I played alto, clarinet, and bass clarinet. So we had a special broadcast one night where I played the three instruments and I did like oodles and oodles on sax, I did the Flight of the Bumblebee on clarinet. And I believe I did something on bass clarinet, I can't remember what it was. But this was to give the composers an idea of my capabilities and so forth and to write around that. Write them difficult, write them easy, make it hard, you know. So we received roughly about 18 concertos. And believe me when I tell you this, this can be verified by some of the boys at ABC. I was on a go seven days a week because I had to prepare every concerto and get it under my fingers with any reason. Because we had to try it. If you can't play something halfway decent, Whiteman would have never been able to tell how it sounded. So we tried some and if it didn't work out, he says let's try the other movement. And if he liked it, we went through it. Well anyway, we went through about 18 of them. And we finally got down to four concertos that we liked. And this one broadcast, I did two on a half hour broadcast, two on another one. And brother, they were tough. I have all the records of them, you know. They were very difficult. So where was I now? What were we talking about the concerto? The concerto notes. Oh yeah, but I went through it. 1950. But the record. Yes, we recorded the concerto for doubles. Manna albums. And here's the first thing that happens. Ralph Herman had been writing for Manna albums, you know, arranging for the singers and so forth. He says, Al, they would like to try something different. They would like to record the concerto for doubles. Fine. We started at midnight at Carnegie Hall with about a 50 piece orchestra. We went to six in the morning. The Oodles and Noodles was done at quarter of six in the morning. Honest to goodness. And in those days, there wasn't any tape. If you broke a record, you had to start the whole thing over again. Well, we made two, three takes. Oodles and Noodles, we made two takes. Because we were running short of time. Well, the album sold pretty good. And after a while, I asked for royalties. And, well, the accountant will be in and so forth. I got a check for about $240. Ralph Herman was conducting at the radio station in Milwaukee. That's where he's from. He says, we'll send Ralph's check. Well, he never got the check. Finally, one partner bought out the other partner. There were four of us, incidentally. We signed it. These are fags. We each put up $1,350 each to make this thing. It cost close to roughly $6,000. That's all. One partner buys the other partner. He puts it under a different label, ARCO label. Then he finally skips to Miami. And if you look at your musician's journal or the local 802 or international musician, you will notice Irving Berman, unfair list, Manor Records, unfair list. Ralph Herman can't even get his masters back. This is what's a heartbreaker. So anyway, about the Columbia album. In 1955, I started to do the concerts. And by that time, they discontinued the Columbia album. This is where they should have kept it up because the kids were asking, where can I get the Columbia album? I said, it's been discontinued. Jack Federson, who's the president now, wrote to Mitch Miller, why don't you release it? Nothing. Now this is 11 years ago. They have the masters right there. It's a sin that money is going to waste. I'm losing money. They're losing money. And they've got the masters there. I worked hard on that album. Have you heard it? The little one, the Columbia contrast? I did Liza, Dark Eyes. The name of the record is? Saxophone Contrast. Contrast. Yeah, Saxophone Contrast.
quotes.caption: Recording 'Concerto for Doubles' at Carnegie Hall in 1950

quotes.text: Al Gallodoro was born in Chicago on June 20, 1913. His virtuosity on the clarinet and saxophone has taken him from the vaudeville theaters of New Orleans to Carnegie Hall, and at age 92 he still enthralls audiences with his musicianship. He received an honorary degree from Hartwick College in September of 2005. Al was interviewed in Oneonta, New York on December 28, 2005. JoAnn Chmielowski, his pianist and manager, also participated in the interview, which was conducted by Monk Rowe, Director of the Fillius Jazz Archive.
quotes.caption: Biographical mention of performing at Carnegie Hall
quotes.doc_title: Al Gallodoro interviewed by Monk Rowe, Oneonta, New York, December 28, 2005
